data.title: BNY Mellon International Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Summary

Global stocks made gains in the final quarter of the year. Continued enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI)-related companies, interest rate cuts from major central banks, including the US Federal Reserve (Fed) and the Bank of England, and an end to the longest shutdown in the US government’s history all boosted investor sentiment. Against this backdrop, although the Fund produced a positive return over the quarter, it lagged the benchmark. There was positive stock selection within the industrials sector, but this was more than offset by negative stock selection in the healthcare and consumer discretionary sectors. Within the financial sector, we reduced exposure to insurance-related businesses and allocated the proceeds to European banks where we have higher conviction of prospects over the medium term. We focus on businesses with strong assets and believe our multidimensional research is a key differentiator, helping us to navigate uncertainty and position portfolios for long-term success.

BNY Mellon International Equity Fund (Class A at NAV)(NIEAX) produced a positive return but underperformed its benchmark index, the MSCI EAFE (“the index”), during the fourth quarter of 2025, with stock selection in healthcare and consumer discretionary detracting.

1 Gross expenses is the total annual operating expense ratio for the fund, before any fee waivers or expense reimbursements. 2 Net Expenses is the total annual operating expense ratio for the fund, after any applicable fee waivers or expense reimbursements. The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms. The fund's investment adviser, BNY Mellon Investment Adviser, Inc., has contractually agreed, until January 31, 2026, to waive receipt of its fees and/or assume the direct expenses of the fund so that the direct expenses of none of the fund's share classes (excluding Rule 12b-1 fees, shareholder services fees, taxes, interest expense, brokerage commissions, commitment fees on borrowings and extraordinary expenses) exceed .77%. On January 31, 2026, BNY Mellon Investment Adviser, Inc. may terminate this expense limitation agreement at any time.

Global equities rose during the fourth quarter as investors welcomed the Fed’s interest rate cuts and the US-China agreed trade deal. Continued excitement about AI initially supported gains in equities, but the recent anxiety about AI-related valuations unnerved investors in November and December. But despite those new fears, the overall sentiment was positive and many stock indices reached record highs during the quarter.

Stock selection in the healthcare and consumer discretionary sectors was negative. This was partially offset by positive stock selection in industrials.

Top 10 Holdings (12/31/25)%

The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to buy or sell a security. Large concentrations can increase share price volatility.

Top Contributors

When looking at individual stocks, the top-performing holdings for the quarter included Barclays and Rexel.

Barclays (BCS) is a global-grade bank. After several years of an operational improvement program, we believe the business is a much-enhanced franchise, with stronger management, improved risk controls, and more robust balance sheet, which investors have begun to recognize.

Rexel (RXLSF) is a French industrial business that provides electrification products and solutions to help make infrastructures more efficient, safe and sustainable. During the quarter, the US government announced a partnership with Hitachi to modernize the power grid, supporting the development of modular nuclear reactors. This improved energy capability is essential for expanding data centers, which are crucial for AI infrastructure.

Top Detractors

Holdings that detracted over the period included Babcock International and Smurfit Westrock.

In the aerospace and defense sector, UK business Babcock International (BCKIF) was affected by growing anticipation of a peace deal in Ukraine. However, this does not change our long-term investment thesis since global military budget increases are expected to continue.

Shares in Smurfit WestRock (SW) weakened, with several companies providing lower guidance, which is affecting the paper and packaging industry. Although there has been continued positive news on supply consolidation, the demand backdrop has remained weak.

Performance of individual stock evaluated is based on total effect. Total effect represents the opportunity cost of an investment manager's investment decisions relative to an overall benchmark, which can include holdings that are not held by the portfolio but contribute to relative portfolio performance.

Market Outlook

Market volatility persists as investors respond to shifting policy signals from the US administration. Shifting regulatory priorities and geopolitical developments continue to influence sentiment and asset prices. Meanwhile, enthusiasm for AI remains a significant driver of global equity markets.

While AI-related optimism has increased share prices in certain sectors, it has also triggered sharp moves in individual stocks due to perceived competitive threats from AI – many of which may ultimately prove overstated.

Monetary policy adds further uncertainty. Central banks are challenging expectations of imminent rate cuts, with inflation proving stubbornly sticky and policy signals remaining unclear.

Despite these challenges, we remain committed to our investment process. We focus on businesses with strong assets, and we believe that our multidimensional research is the key to us to navigating uncertainty and positioning portfolios for long-term success.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, or a summary prospectus, if available, that contains this and other information about a fund, contact your financial professional. For more information, call 1-800-373-9387 or visit bny.com/investments. Read the prospectus carefully before investing. Investors should discuss with their financial professional the eligibility requirements for Class I shares, which are available only to certain eligible investors, and the historical results achieved by the fund’s respective share classes.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Risks

Diversification cannot assure a profit or protect against loss. Equities are subject to market, market sector, market liquidity, issuer, and investment style risks, to varying degrees. Currencies are subject to the risk that those currencies will decline in value relative to a local currency, or, in the case of hedged positions, that the local currency will decline relative to the currency being hedged. Each of these risks could increase the fund's volatility. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries.

The Morgan Stanley Capital International Europe, Australasia, Far East (MSCI EAFE®) Index is a free float-adjusted market capitalization weighted index that is designed to measure equity performance in developed markets excluding the United States and Canada. The index consists of select designated MSCI national developed market indices. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

Definitions

Q is quarter. NAV is Net Asset Value. YTD is Year to Date. FDIC is Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. AI is Artificial intelligence and refers to computer systems that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision making and language translation. Sticky inflation is when prices for certain goods and services, like rent, insurance, and medical care, change very slowly and persist at high levels, even as other economic factors shift.

As of 12/31/25 the companies mentioned represented 8.52% of the fund’s portfolio in the aggregate. The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to buy or sell a particular security. Other holdings may not have performed as well as some of those listed herein. Portfolio composition is subject to change at any time.

data.title: Realty Income: Has The Net Lease Giant Found A New Growth Engine? (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

If you're not familiar with Realty Income (O), then you probably read the title and assumed this was a bearish article. But for those of us familiar with the net lease giant, their latest quarter was nothing new.

Although I have my qualms with Realty Income and prefer other REITs, O continues to do what it was designed to do, deliver steady income. And I don't foresee this changing anytime soon.

In this article, I discuss their latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I'm upgrading the stock and wouldn't be surprised if the REIT saw above $70 in 2026.

Previous Hold Thesis

I last covered Realty Income this past November in an article titled: A Proven Income Generator With Upside Potential.

Despite their attractive yield of 5.52% and double-digit upside to their price target of $63.40, I remained cautious due to economic uncertainty and limited growth potential.

In Q3, O delivered solid results with growth in AFFO and revenue, driven by portfolio diversification and international expansion. Management also raised full-year AFFO guidance.

But I feared muted growth would limit any significant upside potential. Since then, the REIT has proven me wrong, even outperforming the S&P (SP500). The index is up close to 2%, while O is up over 16%.

Business as Usual

Realty Income reported Q4 earnings last week with mixed results. AFFO missed analysts' estimates by a penny, while revenue managed to beat them by $100 million.

For me, I usually expect REITs to beat revenue expectations due to embedded rent escalators. For misses on their bottom line, this could be an indication of tenant troubles.

But for O, Q4 was business as usual. Occupancy actually improved from 98.3% the prior quarter to 98.9%. During the quarter, they divested 115 vacant properties.

For 2025, disposed vacant properties were 334. In total, O sold 425 properties for net proceeds of $744 million. Their rent recapture rate also increased quarter-over-quarter from 103.5% to 104.9%.

As far as investment activity, Realty Income invested $2.4 billion, an acceleration from Q3, where they invested $1.4 billion. This could have something to do with lower base rates, which have seemed to lead to an increase in M&A activity.

And for 2026, I assume O could see more acceleration. With Jerome Powell's term ending in May, I think it's likely to see rate cuts in the back half of the year, which could provide tailwinds for the sector.

This is likely why their initial guidance is higher than 2025, with $8 billion in investments projected. For 2025, investments totaled $6.3 billion. As a result, this will also likely lead to higher AFFO growth.

Management anticipates AFFO in a range of $4.38 - $4.42. Using their midpoint, this puts AFFO growth at 2.8%, higher than 2025's 2.1% growth. Total AFFO came in at $4.28, $0.01 above guidance, and up from $4.19 in 2024.

Finding New Ways for Growth

One of the major gripes O investors have had is their size hindering their growth. And for good reason. For the year, AFFO growth was just 2.1%.

For comparison, peer Agree Realty (ADC) managed to see 4.6%. And for 2026, this is expected to increase to 5.3% using their current midpoint of $4.56.

But Realty Income is coming into the new year with solid momentum. The REIT entered into a partnership with GIC & Hines to build roughly $1.5 billion in industrial properties in the U.S. & Mexico.

In the quarter, management mentioned they also invested $800 million in the Las Vegas City Center, their second joint venture with Blackstone (BX) on the Sin City strip. Earlier, they also launched their U.S. private fund and raised an additional $816.3 million in Q4.

This brought the total to $1.5 billion and is anticipated to close this month with expectations of $1.7 billion total.

While I don't expect these to accelerate AFFO growth significantly, this does give them additional sources of income and keeps the dividend safe. Furthermore, I suspect the REIT will continue partnering with businesses and tap into new markets for sustainable growth.

Well-Covered Dividend

Speaking of dividends, O ended 2025 with a safe AFFO payout ratio of 75.7%. The annualized dividend was $3.24, a 2.9% increase year-over-year. The conservative payout gives them plenty of retained cash flow.

Annualized free cash flow after dividends increased from approximately $830 million in 2024 to $927 million. So, I expect O to continue rewarding shareholders with steady dividend increases for the foreseeable future.

Debt Profile

Realty Income's conservative payout ratio also gives them flexibility for their upcoming debt. For the year, they do have quite a bit of debt maturing at $2.9 billion. Next year is higher with $3.7 billion due. But the REIT has plenty of levers to pull and had robust liquidity of $4.1 billion at the end of Q4.

Most of their debt was also fixed at 93.4%. Net debt to EBITDA was flat year-over-year at 5.4x. Conservative leverage and their A credit rating give O plenty of flexibility should we see increased macro uncertainty.

Upside Potential

In previous articles, I was cautious on Realty Income's limited growth. But recent partnerships and the likelihood of seeing lower base rates lead me to believe they could see share price acceleration in 2026 and beyond.

For starters, as I mentioned previously, I expect multiple rate cuts this year. As a result, investors will likely rotate back into the market looking for higher yields.

And the rotation has already started, as many dividend stocks have outperformed the broader market in the last month or so. Going forward, I think this could continue. And with O being a popular name with a yield close to 5%, the REIT looks poised to benefit.

Moreover, despite recent price appreciation, they're still attractively priced at 15x AFFO. While I prefer the stock in the mid to low $50s, this is slightly below the sector median's 15.74x. Going forward, I anticipate O can trade between 16x and 18x AFFO.

According to FAST Graphs, a midpoint puts the share price close to $80 by the end of 2028, more than 20% upside from the current price.

Risks & Conclusion

Because of O's larger size, I don't foresee many risks for the REIT. But one major risk that could result in underperformance or even a sell-off is if we see a recession in the near term.

With the recent labor market weakening and the threat of continued AI disruption, I do think recession risks are increasing. And we could see one in the next year or two. If so, O may see lower occupancy ratings, impacting overall growth.

They also have significantly less exposure to higher-rated tenants compared to Agree Realty. Investors should keep an eye out for this, as further upticks in unemployment could be an indication of what's to come.

But O's diversified portfolio, robust cash flows, and multiple levers will likely allow them to see minimal impacts. Moreover, the REIT has seemed to find a new growth engine with multiple partnerships that diversify their cash flow streams.

Due to this, tailwinds from the likelihood we see multiple rate cuts, and benefitting from the rotation from fixed-rate investments, I am upgrading Realty Income from a hold to a buy.

data.title: Sea: Investment Case Delayed, Not Derailed (Upgrade To Strong Buy)
data.description:
Introduction

Things have gotten tough for Sea (SE), impacted by a highly competitive e-commerce environment across most of its markets (Southeast Asia and Latin America) due in part as a repercussion of the end of the de minimis tax exception in the US that forced PDD (PDD) and others to shift more aggressively to Sea's markets and also due to a more aggressive Amazon (AMZN), while MercadoLibre (MELI) also reacted to safeguard share. The consumer is the winner. However, while Shopee is suffering, i.e., not growing margins as I expected, Sea's other businesses, gaming and fintech, continue to grow and, after the selloff, make the stock cheap. I upgrade to strong buy with a US$138 YE26 price target.

4Q25 Results

The market has been worried about Shopee's results, with competitors cutting take rates, offering free shipping, and generally trying to gain consumer spending share mostly from brick-and-mortar retailers, which has also hurt e-commerce margins. The 4Q25 results were within the consensus range, but the main pressure point was guidance that Shopee would have similar EBITDA in 2026 to 2025 despite a 25% increase in GMV. This sharp margin cut implication drove the share price correction in my view. However, on a consolidated basis, forward earnings growth is still in the 20% range, as I will detail in the operating model update.

Operating Model Update

The consensus has not yet updated for the company's guidance, so I adjusted the operating model accordingly, cutting the e-commerce take rate and margins that drive EBITDA to be in line with 2025 results. The gaming business continues to grow at 15% annually, with high margins, and is the company's cash cow. The FinTech segment is also growing rapidly, scaling the loan book and keeping debt costs or loan loss provisions stable. These two core businesses may be neglected by the market, but they provide a solid cash flow base as e-commerce continues to grow. I am reminded of the adage about the power of large numbers: while e-commerce has low margins/take rates, GMV approaching $200 billion provides enormous operating leverage, as observed in Amazon and Mercado Libre. The Sea investment case has not been derailed but delayed.

Finally, this is a positive working-capital business given its 3P e-commerce model and asset-light nature, with modest capex relative to sales. This has helped the company remain FCF positive and accumulate over $11 billion in net cash, providing enterprise stability.

Valuation

I initially bought into SEA in May 2025, doubled the position in January, and now doubled that with an average cost of $104. The main risk outlined in my initial report came true: competition has cut into margins and, more so, into valuation multiples. The company will need to deliver on the new guidance and demonstrate resilience before the market can award it with a higher PEG. My new YE26 price target is $136 at a 1.0x PEG or 24x P/CE (I use adjusted earnings or cash earnings, which is GAAP net income, stock-based compensation, and depreciation). This is a cut from 1.5x PEG, although the growth and absolute cash earnings are nearly the same.

Peer Comps

The e-commerce sector has been downgraded, most likely in favor of AI infrastructure plays. The peer comps table below highlights the strong earnings growth of over 27% on average in the YE26/27 period, according to consensus. While valuations are firmly discounted at under 0.8x PEG. Sea is not the cheapest but has above-average growth and still low margins.

Risk

Apart from trade wars and higher input costs driven by logistics/oil, competition is unlikely to let up, and companies with greater scale and know-how can grow and maintain positive margins. Sea should continue to do well, albeit with greater volatility, as it repositions strategies to address competitive threats.

Conclusion

I upgrade to Strong Buy. The company has three successful businesses that generate growth and cash flow. The e-commerce segment, which had promised the greatest margin gains and exceptional growth, is now expected to flatline in 2026, leading to a severe decline in share price. However, in my updated estimates, Sea still has over 20% earnings growth and is now undervalued at 0.6x PEG or 15x P/CE.

data.title: John Hancock Disciplined Value International Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Objective

Long-term growth of capital

Use for

Core international holding

Morningstar category

Foreign Large Value

Quarterly commentary

Highlights

  • The MSCI EAFE Index—the fund’s benchmark—gained ground in the fourth quarter.
  • The fund finished slightly behind the index.
  • The fund outperformed for the full year thanks to strong stock selection across a broad range of sectors.

Market review and outlook

International equities performed very well in the fourth quarter and finished 2025 with their best return in a calendar year since 2003. Notably, the asset class strongly outpaced the United States in both the quarter and the year. The foreign markets were supported by a backdrop of steady economic growth, falling short-term interest rates, and healthy corporate earnings, as well as their attractive valuations relative to the United States. Consistent with the trend that was in place through the first nine months of 2025, the value style outperformed the broader MSCI EAFE Index.

The consensus outlook for international equities remains positive heading into 2026, bolstered by accommodative fiscal, monetary, and regulatory policies. We believe that even if the technology sector loses momentum, international equities could benefit as investors look for ways to broaden their holdings. In our view, this creates a favorable environment for bottom-up stock selection.

Contributors and detractors

The fund’s modest fourth-quarter underperformance was largely the result of adverse selection in the consumer discretionary and energy sectors. The shortfall in the former primarily reflected pullbacks in stocks that had produced strong returns over the course of the year, but where there was no deterioration in fundamentals. At the individual holding level, a position in the Chinese e-commerce company Alibaba Holding Co. Ltd. was the largest detractor. The stock was pressured by softness in China’s equity market more broadly, together with concerns about weaker consumer spending in the country. The fund was also hurt by underperformance for the British aerospace/defense company BAE Systems, Inc., which gave back some ground following a multi-year stretch of robust returns.

On the other hand, the fund generated the widest margin of outperformance in IT, materials, and consumer staples. Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd., was the leading individual contributor in the technology sector and the portfolio as a whole. The company’s shares rose as investors priced in a powerful memory chip upcycle driven by AI demand and higher prices for Samsung’s flash storage products. The pharmaceutical stock AstraZeneca PLC was another top contributor. Its shares rose as positive drug pipeline news and reduced worries about U.S. healthcare policy fueled optimism about the company’s growth outlook.

Portfolio changes

We didn’t make any significant changes in the quarter, but we did shift in a slightly more defensive direction. We reduced holdings in banks that had outperformed in 2025, and we moved the proceeds into more defensive stocks within the financials sector. We also trimmed the fund’s weightings in industrial stocks that had outperformed and boosted its allocation to the healthcare sector. In addition, we made a small reduction to its weighting in consumer staples. Although we see value opportunities in the sector, business momentum is sluggish relative to other areas of the market. The fund remained tilted toward mid-cap stocks over mega caps. This isn’t a top-down call, but rather a reflection of the mispricings we have found in the category.

In terms of geographies, the fund was underweight in Europe, Japan, and Asia ex- Japan at the end of December, and it was overweight in the United Kingdom, North America (primarily Canada), and the emerging markets. The allocation to emerging-market equities was at the high end of the historical range, reflecting the compelling opportunities we continued to identify across the asset class.

This commentary reflects the views of the portfolio managers named and is subject to change as market and other conditions warrant. No forecasts are guaranteed. This commentary is provided for informational purposes only and is not an endorsement of any security, mutual fund, sector, or index. The subadvisor, the advisor (John Hancock Investment Management LLC), and their affiliates, employees, and clients, may hold or trade the securities mentioned in this commentary. Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of a loss.

data.title: Casella Waste Systems: Rating Upgrade As The Next Leg Of Adj. EBITDA Growth Becomes Visible
data.description:
Summary

Following my previous coverage on Casella Waste Systems (CWST), which I downgraded to a hold rating due to my view that the valuation is no longer attractive, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. The business is still executing well, but more importantly, the next leg of upside now looks more tangible. Mid-Atlantic integration is nearing completion, landfill expansion gives CWST a longer growth runway, and M&A remains a meaningful growth lever. With valuation now much lower than last May, I think the hurdle to generate good returns from here is far less demanding, which makes the setup more attractive again.

4Q25 earnings review

CWST delivered $469.1 million in revenue in the latest quarter, a growth of 9.7% y/y. Of that growth, $23.1 million came from acquisitions and $18.5 million came from same-store growth. Within it, Solid Waste revenue saw $376 million, up 9.9% y/y, and Resource Solutions revenue saw $93 million, up 9.1% y/y. Inside Solid Waste, collection revenue was $308 million, 12.3% y/y growth, while disposal revenue was $64 million, up slightly at 0.5%. Collection price increased 4.6%, driven by 5.3% growth in front-load commercial pricing, with collection volume down only 0.3%. I would say that is a decent result, although roll-off remained weak and fell 5.2%. Front-load and residential volumes were also slightly positive.

Disposal seemed weak on the surface, but it is not that bad if we look into the details. The disposal price increased 4.1%, while volume fell 4.5%. But total landfill tons still went up 1.7%, with internalized volumes up by ~10%. The delta is a positive datapoint, as it shows that actual flow through the landfill network improved. Resource Solutions did well. Recycling and other processing revenue fell 1.4% because commodity prices were weaker, but National Accounts revenue went up 15.6%. Processing volume in revenue terms grew 12%, supported by higher volumes at the upgraded Willimantic facility. Average recycled commodity revenue per ton fell 27%, but management said the contract structure enabled them to push much of that pricing pressure back to customers, so the net revenue impact was less than $1 million. In other words, lower commodity prices were a headwind, but not really an earnings problem.

Margins were solid. CWST delivered adj. EBITDA of $107.0 million, up 12.7% y/y, and adj. EBITDA margin expanded 60 bps to 22.8%. That is a very good set of results, especially if we consider new acquisitions diluted margin by 40 bps. Which means, on a core business basis, CWST expanded margin by 100 bps.

Why I am bullish now

The biggest one is the Mid-Atlantic. I believe this is the most important positive point to note, as it gives confidence adj. EBITDA margin can go above the historical threshold of ~23%. CWST received the 40 automated trucks that had been delayed earlier, and management expects those trucks, together with labor efficiencies and route optimization, to generate more than $5 million of savings in 2026. >$5 million doesn’t seem a lot on an absolute basis, but it is another 1% adj. EBITDA margin. CWST has also more or less completed the customer migration from acquired billing systems to the integrated Casella lead-to-cash platform, with the last piece expected by late 1Q or early 2Q. We have yet to see the real tangible impacts from this integration, but I’d expect better routing, better pricing, and less operational friction, which all lead to better profitability. Together, I would expect the adj. EBITDA margin to continue expanding for the years to come.

The next part of the story that I like is the landfill side. According to management, the Hyland permit is expected within the year, and if true, that would increase annual permit capacity from 460,000 tons to 1 million tons and add ~60 years of capacity at current run rates. The Hakes expansion is also expected in the next couple of quarters and would add another 10+ years of capacity. CWST also has the McKean rail upgrade, which is still on track for completion in 2Q26. On one hand, this expansion would enable CWST to move the tons from Ontario County (preparing for end-2028 closure) to another facility (noted to be Hyland). On the other hand, it also gives CWST more room to absorb higher-value waste streams, especially if the buildout in US manufacturing and industrial construction (due to the reshoring goal) really goes into a supercycle, which would naturally result in more C&D (construction and demolition) and related disposal demand.

Lastly, CWST continues to surprise me on the M&A side of things. In 2025, they acquired nine businesses with ~$115 million of annualized revenue (added another $30 million with Mountain State Waste on 1st Jan 2026). That ~$115 million represents ~7% growth (of FY24 revenue), and management said the acquisition pipeline is over $500 million of annualized revenue. If we make a conservative assumption that CWST will only acquire the same ~$115 million of revenue a year, that means CWST can repeat this for the next 4 years or so. Do they have the financial capacity to do it? Yes, CWST leverage ratio is still only at 2.34x leverage, which is much lower than peers like GFL Environmental (GFL), Waste Connections (WCN), and Waste Management (WM), which are >3x. They can easily lever the balance sheet up (draw down the $700 million revolver) to acquire these businesses.

Valuation

Given that valuation has come down by a ton since last May, now at ~14.7x forward EBITDA from 19.3x previously, the underlying assumptions needed to make returns from here are less aggressive. All we need is for CWST to grow adj. EBITDA by 10% for the next few years, which I think is a lower hurdle. Acquired revenues could easily contribute mid-to-high-single-digit growth, coupled with organic growth from capacity expansion and margin expansion from cost savings and efficiencies; 10% is really not tough. If it grows as expected, we don’t even need multiples to expand. Assuming the same 14.7x, we can bridge to 23% upside.

Risk

A key driver for the upside now depends on CWST finishing the Mid-Atlantic system work and turning that into real route, labor, and pricing gains. If that delays or fails to materialize as well as it should, the margin story gets pushed out. Delays at Hyland, Hakes, or the McKean rail project would also drag down the growth story. On top of that, CWST is still buying smaller operators, and those deals usually come in at lower margins first before the margin ramps up, so we could see offsetting margin headwinds in the near term.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for CWST is an upgrade to buy. Not only has the stock gotten cheaper, but the operating story also looks better. Mid-Atlantic should start translating into real savings, landfill expansions create more room for growth, and the acquisition pipeline remains attractive. All in all, I think CWST now has more visible drivers to grow EBITDA over the next few years, and even without aggressive assumptions, investors can make decent returns.

data.title: Harrow: The Post Earnings Drop Exposes The Cost Of Growth (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

My last look at Harrow (HROW) was in August 2025. I have been one of the few analysts on the platform who’s expressed caution. But Harrow’s growth is undeniable. Its YoY revenue growth in the past several quarters has been consistently ≥30%, and 36% YoY in FY25 compared to FY24.

That said, Harrow’s growth has not been “organic,” and it comes with increased costs.

This became very apparent after the company released its Q4/FY25 earnings yesterday—featuring a GAAP EPS miss. Moreover, Q4 revenue of $89M fell merely in the “base band” that I modeled for back in August.

Today, HROW is down around 25% in intraday trading.

The following article takes a closer look at Harrow’s Q4 results to try to find out what’s behind the stock plunge.

Earnings

Back in August, I warned that Harrow’s “>$280M” FY25 guidance was “ambitious” and would “necessitate a dramatic revenue acceleration in the second half of the year.” Management missed the mark—generating just $272.3M in revenue in FY25 (+36% YoY). There was also an asterisk attached to the $272.3M in revenue. Q4 was impacted by elevated customer stocking of IHEEZO. So softer IHEEZO revenue is expected in 1H 2026.

The company admitted to its past missteps in revenue guidance and made an objective “to meet and beat expectations” moving forward. So this is management’s attempt to regain investor trust after missing their own expectations.

The big kicker in Q4 was not “hurt feelings,” but increased costs and interest expense.

It’s easy to forget about Harrow’s relatively high debt load of roughly $243M, but this comes with interest payments. In 2025, Harrow generated $43.9M (cash from operations). While significantly improved from 2024, a large portion of this hard-earned dough is going towards interest expenses. To be clear, this is just the interest. In regards to principal, management “kicked the can” five years down the road after securing 8.625% Senior Notes (due 2030) last year. So while there is no immediate threat of a “liquidity crunch,” there is a real “cost of growth” for Harrow.

Management’s guidance for FY26 is back-loaded: $133M-$153M in the 1H and $203M-$226M in the 2H. This is due to management’s plan to double its sales force for VEVYE and TRIESENCE. You can bet your "bottom dollar" that this will involve increased expenses in the 1H that will, hopefully, pay off in the 2H. Again, the "cost of growth."

Products & Pipeline

As I’ve pointed out in the past, Harrow’s products do not “sell themselves” because, in my view, they are “hardly differentiated” from alternatives to merit organic interest.

Harrow’s "newest toy" is Melt Pharmaceuticals. G-MELT (formerly MELT-300) is a clever combination of midazolam and ketamine being evaluated for sedation. Midazolam was originally approved in 1985. It's a benzodiazepine (the same class as XANAX) that is commonly used for procedural sedation. Ketamine was first approved in 1970.

Harrow is hoping G-MELT’s delivery (sublingual “melt”) will differentiate it. The combination also diminishes the respiratory risks associated with intravenous opioids.

I'll admit that G-MELT is cool—but is it enough to justify a “branded premium?” Another consideration is that the “procedural sedation” market is highly fragmented and these practices have decades of habits/relationships with specific drugs/manufacturers. So I don't think G-MELT is something that will hit the market and instantly become a major contributor for Harrow.

What does this look like when I model it?

I assumed 4M annual cataract procedures (growing at a CAGR of 2%), a 10% peak share, a 75% Probability of Success, and $200 net price per dose. The Sensitivity Analysis—at the bottom of the above image—accounts for variation to peak share and net price assumptions.

G-MELT’s risk-adjusted net present value comes out to be roughly $76M.

We also have to keep in mind that Harrow is essentially cannibalizing a part of its own compounded business to G-MELT. And it’s totally possible that management bundles G-MELT alongside other products (IHEEZO and TRIESENCE) to boost volume. But this would come at the expense of pricing pressure and/or discounts.

Valuation Assumptions

Even after today’s ~25% plunge, HROW still appears priced optimistically per my DCF model.

My DCF model assumes $357.5M in Year 1 Revenue (management's midpoint for FY26 guidance), 20% Revenue Growth (CAGR over the nine-year projection), 15% FCF margin, and a Beta of 1.8 (which is actually lower than HROW’s >2.0 beta per Seeking Alpha).

Financial data—like Debt and Cash—were derived from Harrow’s latest financial statements.

Even with, what I believe to be, optimistic assumptions (20% CAGR is challenging for any company to achieve without ongoing M&A), my model has HROW being overvalued by about 19%.

Given recent trends, my 15% FCF margin assumption may be too optimistic as well.

In the past six quarters, Harrow’s FCF margin has been comfortably below 10% and they are planning for increased opex this year. It’s just not a high-margin business model and they are not (yet) making any obvious operational leverage gains. So my model assumes that Harrow significantly grows into this FCF margin assumption.

One “Bull” case—what HROW appears already priced for—effectively assumes 31% CAGR and 10% FCF margins.

Upside from there requires considerable margin expansion into the low-to-mid teens. The other "Bull" case (2.0) takes it easier on growth expectations (20%) but then would require very high FCF margin (18%) to justify HROW's current valuation.

My Sensitivity Analysis (on the Base case) shows that HROW’s valuation is equally sensitive to growth and margins.

But the “margin of safety” still appears narrow.

Because my DCF model spits out just one result, I like to run a Monte Carlo simulation (on the Base case) to account for variation in key inputs like revenue growth, beta, and terminal growth.

Based on my Base case, HROW could reasonably trade between $20.40 and $51.69 before becoming ridiculously under- or overvalued.

The takeaway from all of this is that HROW is priced aggressively despite the recent drawback. From my porch, HROW could trade down to the low-$20s (barring major changes to base assumptions) and I would “not bat an eye” at it.

Final Words

Per my DCF model, Harrow is still being priced like a "high-margin, high-flying" pharma business when it is becoming increasingly apparent that it is a "low-margin, inorganic growth" business. While management deserves credit for generating cash last year, a large part of it is being consumed by interest payments from the principal that was needed in the first place to drive said growth.

Growth in 2026 and beyond appears largely dependent on S&M investments for existing products (e.g., IHEEZO and TRIESENCE) or newly acquired, branded products (e.g., G-MELT) with uncertain regulatory and commercial prospects.

Because HROW is only merely flat since my last write-up despite earnings challenges (i.e., missing their own revenue expectations for FY25) and it appears valued for unreasonable assumptions, I think a rating downgrade to “Sell” is warranted here.

Risks to my “Sell” rating include an improved financial picture (e.g., revenue acceleration, improved profitability, less debt), G-MELT's FDA-approval (NDA submission planned in 1H 2027) and subsequent outperformance in an established and generic-heavy procedural sedation market, BYOOVIZ and OPUVIZ launch and outperformance in a crowded anti-VEGF biosimilar market, and improved market sentiment.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: CrowdStrike ARR Tops $5.2B
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

CrowdStrike's (CRWD) Q4 financial results demonstrate ARR surpassing $5B. (00:13) U.S. Senate set to vote on Trump’s Iran war decision amid widening conflict. (01:21) Anthropic approaches $20B revenue run rate amid Pentagon clash over AI use. (02:26)

This is an abridged transcript.

CrowdStrike (CRWD) is up 0.7% in premarket action after reporting its fourth quarter fiscal 2026 financial results.

For the quarter ended January 31, the cybersecurity firm reported adjusted earnings per share of $1.12 versus the consensus estimate of $1.10. GAAP EPS was $0.15 compared to the $0.04 estimate.

Revenue for the fourth quarter increased 23% year over year and was just above consensus. Subscription revenue also increased 23% year over year to $1.24B, which was in line with the estimate.

Annual recurring revenue increased 24% year over year to $5.25B.

For the first quarter of fiscal 2027, CrowdStrike expects revenue of $1.36B, which is more than the $1.35B estimate. It expects adjusted EPS of $1.06 to $1.07 compared to the $1.06 estimate.

Here’s a link to the earnings call transcript.

The Senate is set to vote today on President Trump’s decision to launch military action against Iran.

The legislation, known as a war powers resolution, gives lawmakers an opportunity to demand congressional approval before any further attacks are carried out.

Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer said at a news conference Tuesday, “Wars without clear objectives do not remain small. They get bigger, bloodier, longer, and more expensive.” He went on to say, “This is not a necessary war. It’s a war of choice.”

Secretary of State Marco Rubio told reporters in a news conference at the Capitol Tuesday, “We are not going to put American troops in harm’s way.”

So far, six U.S. service members have been killed since the war began. Trump has also not ruled out deploying U.S. ground troops.

The Senate resolution, along with a similar measure expected to be voted on in the House later this week, faces long odds in the Republican-controlled Congress and would almost certainly be vetoed by Trump even if it were to pass.

Anthropic (ANTHRO) is on track to generate nearly $20B in annual revenue, more than doubling its run rate from $9B at the end of 2025.

The AI firm, now valued at about $380B, recently topped $19B in run-rate revenue, which was roughly $14B a few weeks ago, Bloomberg reported, citing sources.

However, its momentum faces uncertainty after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth labeled Anthropic (ANTHRO) a U.S. supply-chain risk following a dispute over restrictions on the Pentagon’s use of its AI for surveillance and autonomous weapons.

More on Anthropic’s outlook in the Wall Street Breakfast newsletter. Here’s a link to signup.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Superintelligence push: Meta said to create applied AI engineering organization

Firearm demand continues its five-year downtrend in the U.S.

OpenAI eyeing contract with NATO, defends Pentagon deal: report

Catalyst watch:

  • Apple (AAPL) has invited press and creators to in-person "Apple Experience" sessions in New York, London, and Shanghai as part of its coordinated announcements of new iPad/Mac products.

  • Lennox (LII) will host its Investor Day event in the Dallas-Fort Worth area. The program will feature formal presentations from top management as they outline the company's operational progress, strategic priorities, and new long-term growth targets.

  • Pentair (PNR) will host its Investor Day event.

  • Netflix (NFLX) CFO Spence Neumann will participate in a Q&A session at the Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 1.1% at $75. Bitcoin is up 4% at $71,000. Gold is up 1.9% at $5,187.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.5% and the DAX is up 1.4%. South Korea's financial markets faced a historic "Black Wednesday" as the KOSPI (KOSPI) fell over 12% in intraday trading, extending a brutal sell-off driven by increased tensions in the Middle East, which led to higher oil prices.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Ross Stores (ROST) +6% - Shares jumped after reporting strong Q4 results and issuing upbeat guidance, as solid holiday sales and stepped-up marketing helped offset tariff pressures and softer consumer sentiment.

Economic calendar:

  • 7:00 am MBA Mortgage Applications

  • 8:15 am ADP Employment Report

data.title: Chart Of The Day: What's Moving Now - And What Might Happen Next
data.description:
By Mike Larson

The record of geopolitical conflicts is clear: Markets can and will get past them... eventually. But the road between Point A to Point B can be volatile and financially treacherous. So, what’s moving now - and what might happen next?

Let’s start with my MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the performance of several ETFs over the past five trading days. They include ones that track key stock market sectors, like the US Global JETS ETF (JETS) and the Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF (PPA), and funds that track commodities, including the United States Oil Fund (USO) and United States Natural Gas Fund (UNG).

From Oil Prices to Airline Stocks, this Conflict is Hitting Home

(Data by YCharts)

The short-term impacts are abundantly clear - and in many cases, logical. USO up more than 9%, PPA up 3.6%, and JETS down 4% “make sense” given the threat to energy supplies, the likelihood of increased defense spending, and the large disruptions to global travel.

In other cases, they seem odd. The iShares MSCI Israel ETF (EIS), for instance, is up 3.8% despite the fact the nation is facing Iranian counterattacks. Then you have the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) unchanged. Treasuries have historically seen big “flight to safety” flows during global conflicts. But that isn’t happening this time around - likely because of fears higher energy prices will push already simmering inflation higher.

Which of these moves will stick for more than a few days? And how should you trade them? It all comes down to several questions, including...

  1. How long will the conflict last?
  2. What will the endgame be?
  3. Which energy facilities and supply routes will be targeted or disrupted?
  4. How will that impact oil, gas, and product prices... as well as inflation?
  5. How will monetary policymakers and businesses react - both here in the US and abroad?

The answers aren’t clear, as the conflict is in flux. But the sectors and funds I highlight here are the ones to watch - or trade as new developments unfold, if you’re so inclined.

As a long-term investor, you can take heart from one thing. No matter the conflict, no matter the result, markets ultimately found their footing after fights both large and small. So, before you make large-scale, big-picture strategy shifts, try to keep that in mind.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Invesco Core Plus Bond Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Potential to lock in yields

Bond yields, though down from their highs, are still at attractive levels compared to the last 10 years, in our view. Investors can lock in these yields for years to come by investing in high grade bonds before interest rates come down further.

2 Picking your spots

We believe numerous important factors enhance the attractiveness of investment grade credit. We observe that yield spreads between Treasuries and non-Treasury sectors have been tight, which makes our credit underwriting and security selection even more important.

3 Beating the crowd

Investors have amassed large amounts in cash and money markets (Source: St. Louis Fed. Data as of 12/31/25). As US Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cutting resumes and interest rates fall, cash will likely move to risk assets, pushing bond prices up and yields down. Timing a move is hard, but for now, investors are in our view being paid to wait in fixed income.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Market sentiment during the quarter appeared mixed. The US unemployment rate rose and consumer spending growth appears to have slowed, both likely affected by the longest government shutdown in US history. Still, overall economic growth was buoyed by investment in artificial intelligence ('AI') and related sectors. Corporate fundamentals remained firm following a strong earnings season and elevated capital market activity. However, a divergence of economic confidence between CEOs and consumers raised concerns about an economic divide between higher and lower income earners. AI “bubble” fears appeared to further dampen sentiment.
  • A weakening labor market led the Fed to resume rate cuts, reducing the federal funds rate twice, for a target range of 3.50-3.75%, the lowest since 2022. Markets expect two more rate cuts in 2026 as the Fed has expressed a desire to get interest rates close to a neutral stance. Credit spreads – the yield difference between Treasuries and US investment grade bonds – widened due to moderate volatility early in the quarter but have remained historically tight.
  • Our bond outlook is constructive. Market-implied recession probabilities in our view remain well below the expectations of surveyed economists, and interest rate stability, if it materializes, would likely further support demand. Our view is that growth will accelerate in 2026 but might face a headwind of elevated inflation early in the year.

Portfolio characteristics*

Fund Index
Effective duration (yrs) 6.08 5.64
Coupon (%) 4.76 3.68
30-day SEC yield (Class A shares) 3.92 -
30-day SEC unsubsidized yield (Class A shares) 3.90 -

Investment categories (%)

Portfolio Index
Securitized 54.67 26.15
MBS 31.65 24.26
ABS 15.86 0.45
CMBS 7.16 1.44
Corporate Bonds 27.33 20.70
US Investment Grade Bonds 19.54 20.68
US High Yield Bonds 7.79 0.02
Non-US Debt 15.59 6.31
Emerging Market Debt 6.12 1.17
Non-US Investment Grade Bonds 5.79 3.05
Sovereign Debt 2.00 2.09
Non-US High Yield Bonds 1.67 0.00
Government Bonds 14.64 45.63
US Treasuries 14.64 45.63
Convertible Bonds 1.32 0.00
Municipal Bonds 0.01 0.65
Derivatives & FX 0.02 0.00
Others 1.66 0.57

Portfolio positioning

We maintain a positive outlook for investment grade credit and maintain the fund's overweight in the sector. Yield spreads between Treasuries and investment grade bonds have remained tight compared to past levels, but elevated yields appear to have continued to attract buyers. Based on the segment's health, we believe investment grade corporates remain attractive on a risk-adjusted basis. In our view, corporate fundamentals remain firm, the yield backdrop appears attractive and lower average dollar prices for bonds across the index present discounted buying opportunities, potentially enhancing downside protection for bondholders.

As the Fed's rate cutting has resumed, our perspective is that, in the current environment, the bond market benefits from rate cuts regardless of what drives them. If the Fed cuts because inflation has sustainably cooled, yield spreads between Treasuries and US investment grade bonds could remain tight or even compress further. Conversely, if cuts come in response to weaker growth, investment grade credit should still hold up – high-quality bonds generally become more valuable and income remains durable. Therefore, we have a constructive view of bonds in the intermediate part of the yield curve, which captures income and price appreciation potential while reducing excess duration risk.

Within the investment grade corporate segment, we maintain an overweight in financials, particularly in the banking sector. While valuations in the sector have narrowed toward long-term median levels, we believe the strength of corporate fundamentals still supports a positive outlook.

During the quarter, we decreased the fund's overweights in the high-yield corporate and emerging market debt segments, which are not in the benchmark, in order to reduce credit risk and in anticipation of a potentially robust new issuance calendar. Yields and income are in our view attractive; however, we believe diligence is critical given potential for heightened volatility. Additionally, we maintain an underweight in Agency mortgages because valuations have become less attractive to us.

We kept the fund's duration fairly neutral compared to the benchmark, lengthening it due to potential slowing of US growth and as a hedge to credit risk.

Performance highlights

Detractors from performance

Contributors to performance

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US investment grade, fixed-rate bond market. An investment cannot be made directly in an index.

About Risk

Active trading results in added expenses and may result in a lower return and increased tax liability.

An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer's credit rating.

Derivatives may be more volatile and less liquid than traditional investments and are subject to market, interest rate, credit, leverage, counterparty, and management risks. An investment in a derivative could lose more than the cash amount invested.

Environmental, Social and Governance ('ESG') considerations may vary across investments and issuers, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated for investment. The Fund will not be solely based on ESG considerations; therefore, issuers may not be considered ESG-focused companies. ESG factors may affect the Fund's exposure to certain companies or industries and may not work as intended. The Fund may underperform other funds that do not assess ESG factors or that use a different methodology to identify and/or incorporate ESG factors. ESG is not a uniformly defined characteristic and as a result, information used by the Fund to evaluate such factors may not be readily available, complete or accurate, and may vary across providers and issuers. There is no guarantee that ESG considerations will enhance Fund performance.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers, including emerging markets, can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa.

Junk bonds have greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer's credit quality. Junk bond values fluctuate more than high quality bonds and can decline significantly over a short time.

The investment techniques and risk analysis used by the portfolio managers may not produce the desired results.

Mortgage- and asset-backed securities are subject to prepayment or call risk, which is the risk that the borrower's payments may be received earlier or later than expected due to changes in prepayment rates on underlying loans. Securities may be prepaid at a price less than the original purchase value.

Obligations issued by US Government agencies and instrumentalities may receive varying levels of support from the government, which could affect the fund's ability to recover should they default.

The Fund is subject to certain other risks. Please see the current prospectus for more information regarding the risks associated with an investment in the Fund.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund's portfolio management, are based on current market conditions and are subject to change without notice. These opinions may differ from those of other Invesco investment professionals.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Note: Not all products available at all firms. Financial professionals, please contact your home office.

Ratings source: Standard & Poor's (SPGI), Moody's or Fitch, as applicable. A credit rating is an assessment provided by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization ('NRSRO') of the creditworthiness of an issuer with respect to debt obligations, including specific securities, money market instruments or other debts. Ratings are measured on a scale that generally ranges from AAA (highest) to D (lowest); ratings are subject to change without notice. Not Rated indicates the debtor was not rated and should not be interpreted as indicating low quality. A negative in Cash indicates fund activity that has accrued or is pending settlement. For more information on rating methodologies, please visit the following NRSRO websites:

S&P Global Ratings and select 'Understanding Credit Ratings' under Rating Resources 'About Ratings' on the homepage; What Is a Credit Rating? | Understanding Credit Ratings - Moody's and select 'Understanding Ratings' on the homepage; Fitch Ratings: Credit Ratings & Analysis For Financial Markets and select 'Ratings Definitions Criteria' under 'Resources' on the homepage. Then select 'Rating Definitions' under 'Resources' on the 'Contents' menu.

The fund holdings are organized according to the Global Industry Classification Standard, which was developed by and is the exclusive property and service mark of MSCI Inc. (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's.

* Effective duration is the managers' estimate of a bond fund's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. This measure takes into account mortgage prepayments, puts, adjustable coupons and potential call dates. Contribution to Return measures the performance impact from portfolio holdings over a defined time period. It takes into account both weight and performance of the portfolio holdings. Contribution to Return is calculated at security level.

Morningstar

Source: ©2025 Morningstar Inc. All rights reserved. The information contained herein is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers. It may not be copied or distributed and is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Open-end mutual funds and exchange-traded funds are considered a single population for comparison purposes. Had fees not been waived and/or expenses reimbursed currently or in the past, the ranking would have been lower. Rankings for other share classes may differ due to different performance characteristics.

Before investing, consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. Visit Country Splash for a prospectus/summary prospectus containing this information. Read it carefully before investing.

data.title: GE Vernova: Repowering America
data.description:
GE Vernova (GEV) is positioned to accelerate growth in the coming years in Power and Electrification as market demand drives substantial growth in new, margin-accretive orders. In addition to expanding production capacity, GE Vernova should see strong tailwinds for profitability over the coming years as the firm expands its serviceable power and electrical equipment footprint, driving recurring revenue through long-term service agreements. With new gas turbine capacity expected to come online in eq3’26 and the addition of Prolec GE to Electrification, I believe GE Vernova is well positioned to realize durable growth over the coming years; I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for GEV shares with a price target of $1,169/share at 59.15x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

GE Vernova reported an upbeat close to FY25 with a 50% sequential increase in quarterly orders, ending q4’25 with $22.26b in new orders for a total book-to-bill ratio of 2.03x; GE Vernova closed FY25 with $150b in backlog. Growth in orders was underpinned by strength in the Electrification and Gas markets, with Electrification printing a book-to-bill ratio of 2.51x. The Wind segment even realized revitalization in the quarter with a book-to-bill ratio of 1.33x, up from a declining order book since q3’24.

GE Vernova Operational Update

Power Segment

Orders in the power segment increased by 72% sequentially to $3.15b for a book-to-bill ratio of 1.33x, adding 6GW of capacity to the backlog for 25.7GW of new orders throughout FY25. In total, GE Vernova has 83GW in slot reservations for gas turbines driven by strong domestic demand. Along with infrastructure sales, GE Vernova is realizing strong services attachment that should drive long-term, margin-accretive growth for the market segment. Supporting growth, GE Vernova’s facility expansion support increased gas turbine yields beginning in eq3'26.

Wind Segment

The Wind segment experienced a strong increase in new orders in q4’25, likely driven by a pull-forward by customers seeking to establish wind infrastructure before the end of 2027 when renewable tax credits are set to sunset. The market segment faced some operational headwinds in q4’25 as a result of the halting of domestic offshore wind projects, resulting in additional accrual costs associated with the delayed construction. At the end of January 2026, a federal judge approved the continuation of the project. given the near completion of the project with only 10 turbine blades and 1 turbine needing installation, I believe GE Vernova should realize incremental margin improvements in eq2’26 as the organization phases out offshore wind in favor of onshore wind projects.

For eFY26, revenue in the Wind segment is expected to decline sharply in e1h26 before recovering in e2h26 driven by higher volumes and potential pricing power driven by contractual obligations relating to tariff pricing that will unfavorably impact e1h26. Looking beyond eFY26, I believe Wind should turn profitable as GE Vernova implements more dynamic pricing into contractual agreements, offsetting variable costs like tariffs and inflationary pressures.

Electrification Segment

Electrification was by far the leading growth driver with $7.4b in new orders for a book-to-bill ratio of 2.51x in q4’25, growing the backlog to $35b. Electrification growth was largely driven by investments by power infrastructure for utilities and data center equipment for the hyperscalers in the US.

On February 2, 2026, GE Vernova announced the completion of the acquisition of the remaining 50% equity interest in Prolec GE for $5.275b, enhancing its internal operations in the electrification market. Accordingly, GE Vernova is expecting to issue $2.6b in debt to finance the acquisition, though will likely maintain a healthy balance sheet given the $9b in cash on the balance sheet at the close of q4’25.

Electrification is set to experience substantial growth in eFY26 following the Prolec GE acquisition, adding roughly $3b in net revenue post-acquisition. In addition to this, management is expecting to double transformer and switchgear production in the coming years to better service heightened market demand, creating substantial growth tailwinds for the Electrification segment.

As a result of the tight market for electrical equipment, GE Vernova should realize appealing pricing power that should convert into revenue beginning in eFY27, driving margins for both Power and Electrification.

Tying it all together, I believe GE Vernova is positioned to realize substantial growth over the coming years as gas turbine slots are about sold out through 2029 and are expected to be sold out through 2030 when the backlog reaches 100GW of capacity. Beyond equipment sales, I believe turbine sales will deliver long-term, margin-accretive services agreements that can provide durable top-line growth while managing costs through inflationary escalators to protect margins. In addition to this, GE Vernova is in a unique position to cross-sell electrical equipment to essentially provide a nearly complete microgrid for hyperscaler power demand as well as provide a near complete offering for utilities.

GE Vernova Financial Forecast

Looking ahead to eq1’26, I’m forecasting GE Vernova to generate $9.2b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $2.03/share. Performance in the quarter will be underpinned by strength in Power and Electrification, which will include 2 months of Prolec GE performance, partially offset by headwinds resulting from the offshore wind project. For eFY26, I’m expecting GE Vernova to experience a strong uplift in adjusted EBITDA margins driven by pricing power for both Power and Electrification as well as improvements in the Wind segment following the completion of its offshore projects. Though I’m expecting Wind to substantially lag the other two reporting segments, I’m expecting incremental improvements as dynamic variable pricing comes into play.

GE Vernova closed FY25 with $9b in cash on the balance sheet and no debt, though investors should note that GE Vernova is expecting to issue $2.6b in debt in eq1’26 to partially finance the Prolec GE acquisition.

Risks Related to GE Vernova

Bull Case

GE Vernova provides a comprehensive service offering, including power generation, electrical infrastructure, and long-term margin-accretive equipment servicing. With strong demand for power and electrification infrastructure, GE Vernova is in a unique position of pricing power and the ability to increase production volumes in order to service demand.

Bear Case

GEV shares are trading at a substantial multiple when considering shares’ historical trading range. GE Vernova may face elongated headwinds for its offshore wind projects that may continue to pressure Wind margins for the coming quarters.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Management outlined a robust outlook for enhancing shareholder value for 2026, including a robust $10b share repurchase authorization along with doubling the dividend when compared to the previous fiscal year.

GEV shares are currently trading at a substantial premium at 72.81x EV/aEBITDA; however, I believe shares are priced based on future growth which entails increased gas turbine production capacity, increased electrical equipment production, and offloading offshore wind, all supported by orders strength and a robust backlog. Given the strong outlook, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for GEV shares with a price target of $1,169/share at 59.15x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Multiples Model Description

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

DCF Model Description

The DCF model can be read with the array of discount rates at the top and terminal growth rates to the left of the model. The forecasted cash flows are denoted at the bottom of the model. The share price array ties the respective discount rates and terminal growth rates. The highlighted prices denoted in the model associate with my expected range for intrinsic value per share.
